quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Force-time graph"?

options.text: W_nc = ΔKE + ΔPE = E_final - E_initial; work by friction or applied forces that changes the total mechanical energy
F_net = ma or F_net = dp/dt; the net force equals mass times acceleration, or equivalently the rate of change of momentum
A system of two masses connected by a string over a pulley; a = (m₁ - m₂)g/(m₁ + m₂) for an ideal massless pulley
The area under F(t) vs. t represents the impulse delivered; larger area means greater momentum change

quiz_questions.explanation: "Force-time graph" is defined as: The area under F(t) vs. t represents the impulse delivered; larger area means greater momentum change. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Average force"?

options.text: x(t) = x₀ + ∫v(t)dt; integrating velocity recovers position, with the constant of integration set by initial conditions
ω = √(k/m) = 2πf = 2π/T; determines how fast the oscillation occurs
F_c = mv²/r; the net radial force needed to maintain circular motion; always directed toward the center
F_avg = Δp/Δt = J/Δt; the constant force that would deliver the same impulse over the same time interval

quiz_questions.explanation: "Average force" is defined as: F_avg = Δp/Δt = J/Δt; the constant force that would deliver the same impulse over the same time interval. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Variable mass systems"?

options.text: F_ext = dp/dt applied to rockets: thrust = v_exhaust × |dm/dt|, where mass decreases as fuel is expelled
When two waves overlap, the resultant displacement is the sum of individual displacements; leads to interference and standing waves
p = mv; a vector quantity equal to mass times velocity, measured in kg·m/s
In collisions, impulsive forces are so large and brief that other forces (gravity, friction) are negligible during the collision

quiz_questions.explanation: "Variable mass systems" is defined as: F_ext = dp/dt applied to rockets: thrust = v_exhaust × |dm/dt|, where mass decreases as fuel is expelled. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Momentum and Impulse, which best defines "Linear momentum"?

options.text: s = rθ; the arc length swept by a point at radius r when the object rotates through angle θ (in radians)
I = Σm_i r_i²; sum of each particle's mass times the square of its distance from the rotation axis
p = mv; the product of mass and velocity; a conserved quantity in isolated systems
A mass m on a spring with constant k oscillates with ω = √(k/m) and T = 2π√(m/k)

quiz_questions.explanation: In Momentum and Impulse, "Linear momentum" refers to: p = mv; the product of mass and velocity; a conserved quantity in isolated systems.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Momentum and Impulse, which best defines "Impulse"?

options.text: J = ∫F dt = Δp; the time integral of force equals the change in momentum
The net impulse on an object equals its change in momentum: J_net = p_final - p_initial
x(t) = x₀ + ∫v(t)dt; integrating velocity recovers position, with the constant of integration set by initial conditions
sinθ ≈ θ (in radians) for small angles; this linearizes the restoring torque and produces SHM

quiz_questions.explanation: In Momentum and Impulse, "Impulse" refers to: J = ∫F dt = Δp; the time integral of force equals the change in momentum.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The area under F(t) vs. t represents the impulse delivered; larger area means greater momentum change"?

options.text: Center of mass motion
Normal force on incline
Critical speed
Force-time graph

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Force-time graph".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "F_avg = Δp/Δt = J/Δt; the constant force that would deliver the same impulse over the same time interval"?

options.text: Average force
Coefficient of friction
Restoring torque
Velocity and acceleration in SHM

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Average force".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "F_ext = dp/dt applied to rockets: thrust = v_exhaust × |dm/dt|, where mass decreases as fuel is expelled"?

options.text: Gravitational PE (universal)
Angular impulse
Variable mass systems
Coefficient of restitution

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Variable mass systems".

quiz_questions.question_text: In Momentum and Impulse, which term matches: "p = mv; the product of mass and velocity; a conserved quantity in isolated systems"?

options.text: Total energy in orbit
Energy conservation in orbits
Potential energy
Linear momentum

quiz_questions.explanation: In Momentum and Impulse, this refers to "Linear momentum".

quiz_questions.question_text: In Momentum and Impulse, which term matches: "J = ∫F dt = Δp; the time integral of force equals the change in momentum"?

options.text: Coefficient of friction
Impulse
Cross product
Center of mass reference frame

quiz_questions.explanation: In Momentum and Impulse, this refers to "Impulse".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Momentum and Impulse is accurate?

options.text: Momentum and Impulse are deeply connected: the impulse (J = ∫F dt) delivered to an object equals its change in momentum (Δp = mΔv).
Forces and Motion applies Newton's laws to systems with multiple forces, including tension, normal force, applied forces, and weight.
A force is conservative if the work it does depends only on initial and final positions, not the path.
Pendulums are oscillating systems where gravity provides the restoring torque.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Momentum and Impulse as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Momentum and Impulse?

options.text: The simple pendulum (point mass on a massless string) has period T = 2π√(L/g) for small angles, independent of mass and amplitude.
For variable forces, the impulse is the area under the force-time graph.
The potential energy function U(x) stores energy that can be recovered as kinetic energy.

Conservation of momentum applies to collisions, explosions, and recoil in one and two dimensions, and is particularly powerful when forces are impulsive (large and brief) or when the system is isolated.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Momentum and Impulse as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Momentum and Impulse?

options.text: Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) occurs when the restoring force is proportional to displacement: F = -kx, producing sinusoidal oscillations x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ).
In AP Physics C, the calculus formulation F = dp/dt can be applied to systems with time-varying forces, including rocket propulsion where mass changes continuously.
Universal Gravitation states that every mass attracts every other mass with force F = Gm₁m₂/r², where G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg².
Work and Kinetic Energy defines work rigorously using calculus: W = ∫F·dr, the line integral of force along the displacement path.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Momentum and Impulse as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Momentum and Impulse?

options.text: Work and Energy provide a scalar framework for analyzing motion without tracking forces and accelerations over time.
Multiple-force problems require careful free-body analysis at specific positions.
A force is conservative if the work it does depends only on initial and final positions, not the path.
Momentum and Impulse are deeply connected: the impulse (J = ∫F dt) delivered to an object equals its change in momentum (Δp = mΔv).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Momentum and Impulse as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Momentum and Impulse?

options.text: Friction problems often involve inclined planes, circular motion, and connected bodies.
A force is conservative if the work it does depends only on initial and final positions, not the path.
For variable forces, the impulse is the area under the force-time graph.
For constant angular acceleration, the rotational kinematic equations parallel their linear counterparts.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Momentum and Impulse as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Momentum and Impulse?

options.text: Calculating the mass of Earth from the known value of g at the surface and Earth's radius: M = gR²/G
Automotive crash testing measuring force-time profiles with accelerometers to calculate impulse and evaluate occupant protection systems
Helicopter tail rotor design: compensating for the angular momentum of the main rotor to prevent the helicopter body from spinning in the opposite direction
Analyzing car crash data using kinematic equations to calculate braking distances and deceleration rates for traffic safety engineering

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Momentum and Impulse.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Momentum and Impulse?

options.text: Space elevator feasibility studies computing the potential energy change of lifting payload from Earth's surface to geosynchronous altitude versus traditional rocket propulsion
Baseball physics analyzing the impulse of a bat on a ball from high-speed camera force-time data to optimize hitting mechanics
Communication satellite placement in geosynchronous orbit at r ≈ 42,200 km ensuring 24-hour period for continuous coverage of a fixed ground region
High jump technique (Fosbury Flop) allows the athlete's center of mass to pass below the bar even as their body clears it

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Momentum and Impulse.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Momentum and Impulse applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Rocket equation derivation: integrating F = dp/dt for variable mass to find the Tsiolkovsky rocket equation Δv = v_e × ln(m₀/m_f)
Molecular chemistry modeling interatomic bonds as potential energy curves (Lennard-Jones potential) to predict bond length and bond energy
Sports biomechanics analyzing the impulse delivered by a bat to a baseball using force-time graphs from high-speed sensors
High jump technique (Fosbury Flop) allows the athlete's center of mass to pass below the bar even as their body clears it

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Momentum and Impulse.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Momentum and Impulse?

options.text: Foucault pendulum demonstration proving Earth's rotation by observing the slow precession of a long pendulum's swing plane over hours
Automotive crash testing measuring force-time profiles with accelerometers to calculate impulse and evaluate occupant protection systems
Vehicle accident reconstruction using partially inelastic collision models and coefficient of restitution to determine pre-collision speeds
Structural engineering calculating forces on bridge components using free-body diagrams and Newton's laws to ensure safe load-bearing capacity

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Momentum and Impulse.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Momentum and Impulse in practice?

options.text: Calculating the mass of Earth from the known value of g at the surface and Earth's radius: M = gR²/G
Automotive airbag design increasing collision time to reduce peak force on occupants while the same impulse (momentum change) occurs
Baseball physics analyzing the impulse of a bat on a ball from high-speed camera force-time data to optimize hitting mechanics
Roller coaster engineering ensuring the car speed at the top of a loop exceeds √(gr) so riders maintain contact with their seats

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Momentum and Impulse.
